canonical_summary:
The FAA's statutory clock for a Part 450 launch or reentry license is 180 days, set by 51 U.S.C. 50905(a)(1) and incorporated into 14 CFR 413.15. That clock only starts after the FAA formally accepts the application as sufficiently complete under 14 CFR 413.11. Pre-application consultation under 14 CFR 413.5 is mandatory, uncapped, and where most of the calendar actually goes. FAA Associate Administrator Kelvin Coleman has cited an average post-acceptance issuance of 151 days, with the agency hitting the 180-day target roughly 98 percent of the time when measured from acceptance forward.

In practice, total wall-clock time from kickoff to a Part 450 vehicle operator license has ranged from roughly 6 to 8 months for the first applicant (Astra, license issued February 4, 2022) to 18 to 24 months or longer for novel vehicles. Blue Origin's New Glenn license issued in 114 days post-acceptance on December 27, 2024. SpaceX's Starship VOL 23-129 issued April 14, 2023, six days before IFT-1, and has since cycled through repeated modifications. The Commercial Space Federation testified on September 10, 2024 that pre-application can take "years" for new entrants like Stoke Nova, Rocket Lab Neutron, and Relativity Terran R.

The binding constraints are means-of-compliance acceptance under 14 CFR 450.35, flight safety analysis under 14 CFR 450.115, and NEPA environmental review under 14 CFR 450.47. The FAA Part 450 SpARC delivered its consensus reform report in late summer 2025; Executive Order 14335, signed August 13, 2025, directs the Secretary of Transportation to reevaluate, amend, or rescind Part 450 requirements not justified by actual launch or reentry risk.

faqs.question: How long does an FAA Part 450 launch license actually take?
faqs.answer: The statutory clock under 51 U.S.C. 50905 is 180 days from FAA acceptance of the application, and FAA Associate Administrator Kelvin Coleman has cited an average post-acceptance issuance of 151 days with a 98 percent on-time rate. In practice, end-to-end wall-clock time from kickoff to license has ranged from roughly 6 to 8 months for Astra in 2021 to 2022, to 12 to 24 months or more for novel vehicles. Blue Origin's New Glenn took 114 days post-acceptance, issued December 27, 2024. The Commercial Space Federation testified that pre-application consultation under 14 CFR 413.5 can take years for new entrants.

faqs.question: When does the 180-day clock start?
faqs.answer: The 180-day clock starts only when the FAA formally accepts the application under 14 CFR 413.11, not when it is filed. The FAA must notify the applicant in writing of acceptance or rejection with each reason. AC 413.13-1, issued December 18, 2023, defines what the agency considers a complete application. Pre-application consultation under 14 CFR 413.5 is mandatory but uncapped and does not count against the clock. If accepted material is later found insufficient, 14 CFR 413.15 lets the FAA toll the clock until the missing information arrives, which is how nominally on-time licenses can still drift months past targets.

faqs.question: What does pre-application consultation involve?
faqs.answer: Under 14 CFR 413.5, an applicant must consult with the FAA before filing. The Part 450 Pre-Application Consultation Checklist scopes the concept of operations, vehicle and payload hazards, trajectory parameters, program schedule, proposed means of compliance, license scope, safety element approvals, any incremental review request, and a compliance plan mapping each Part 450 requirement to a deliverable. AST runs this under internal procedure P-011. Several means of compliance, including those under 14 CFR 450.115, 450.139, 450.145, 450.163, and 450.187, must be accepted by the Administrator before the application can be accepted, which is why this phase routinely takes 12 to 24 months for new entrants.

faqs.question: What is a Request for Additional Information and how many should I expect?
faqs.answer: An RAI is a formal FAA request for documentation or analysis the agency considers necessary to continue review under 14 CFR 413.15. The FAA does not publish per-applicant RAI counts. Deputy Associate Administrator Michael O'Donnell told a House subcommittee in November 2023 that 4 of the first Part 450 licenses required "substantial iterations." Commercial Space Federation president Dave Cavossa testified September 10, 2024 that pre-application is "an endless back and forth process" lasting years for some companies. Every Part 450 applicant has effectively received multiple RAI rounds in pre-application and at least one formal round after acceptance.

faqs.question: Why do means-of-compliance reviews cause so many RAIs?
faqs.answer: Part 450 is performance-based, so an applicant must show how it meets a safety outcome rather than follow a prescriptive recipe. Under 14 CFR 450.35, five categories of means of compliance require Administrator acceptance before application acceptance: flight safety analysis methods under 450.115(b)(1), toxic hazards under 450.139(e)(1), highly reliable flight safety system under 450.145(b), lightning hazard mitigation under 450.163(a)(1), and ground toxic hazards under 450.187(e)(1). Where no Advisory Circular yet defines an accepted method, the applicant proposes one, the FAA asks for substantiation, and the cycle repeats. Former AST head Wayne Monteith called this "ambiguity in what it takes to meet the requirements."

faqs.question: How does environmental review affect the timeline?
faqs.answer: Under 14 CFR 450.47, the FAA must decide whether the action requires a categorical exclusion, written re-evaluation, Environmental Assessment, or Environmental Impact Statement under NEPA. Scope changes reset analysis. SpaceX publicly attributed the slip of Starship Flight 5 from August to October 12, 2024 to Endangered Species Act Section 7 reconsultations with NMFS and USFWS over the hot-stage ring and tower-catch profile. The Starship Programmatic EA took from a September 17, 2021 draft to a June 13, 2022 Mitigated FONSI and ROD, and the Tiered EA for expanded operations took roughly 18 months from public draft to a May 6, 2025 FONSI.

faqs.question: How fast have actual Part 450 licenses been issued?
faqs.answer: Astra received the first Part 450 license on February 4, 2022 for Rocket 3.3 from CCSFS LC-46, roughly 6 to 8 months after pre-application began. SpaceX received Starship VOL 23-129 on April 14, 2023, six days before IFT-1. Blue Origin's New Glenn license issued December 27, 2024 in 114 days from acceptance, with first flight January 16, 2025. Rocket Lab disclosed its Neutron application cleared the preliminary acceptance gate by August 8, 2025. As of June 2025 the FAA had issued 8 Part 450 licenses and denied 1; by the March 9, 2026 transition deadline, the agency reported 14 Part 450 licenses issued since 2021.

faqs.question: How can a builder compress the timeline?
faqs.answer: Start pre-application consultation under 14 CFR 413.5 as early as possible and use the published Part 450 Pre-Application Consultation Checklist as the agenda. File proposed means of compliance under 14 CFR 450.35 against existing Advisory Circulars wherever possible: AC 450.103-1, AC 450.109-1, AC 450.115-2, AC 450.117, AC 450.131-1, AC 450.139-1, AC 450.145, AC 450.169-1A, and AC 450.173-1. Build the application to AC 413.13-1 so the acceptance review under 14 CFR 413.11 succeeds on the first pass. Request incremental review under 14 CFR 450.33 if modules can be reviewed independently. Front-load NEPA scope under 14 CFR 450.47 so later flight profile changes do not reset analysis.

faqs.question: What did Executive Order 14335 change?
faqs.answer: Executive Order 14335, signed August 13, 2025, directs the Secretary of Transportation to reevaluate, amend, or rescind Part 450 requirements not justified by actual launch or reentry risk within 120 days, that is by December 11, 2025. It directs consideration of exemptions for vehicles with flight termination or automated flight safety systems and for vehicles holding FAA airworthiness certificates, broader reentry reliability criteria, and elimination or acceleration of environmental reviews not required by statute. It pairs with the Part 450 SpARC consensus report delivered in late summer 2025. As of June 2026 no Notice of Proposed Rulemaking implementing the Part 450 rewrite has been published in the Federal Register.

faqs.question: Did the March 10, 2026 transition deadline change anything?
faqs.answer: Yes. As of March 10, 2026 all legacy Part 415, 417, 431, and 435 licenses expired, and every U.S. orbital launch or reentry operator must hold a Part 450 license. The FAA confirmed full transition on March 17, 2026. Operators that transitioned include Blue Origin New Shepard and New Glenn, Firefly Alpha, SpaceX Falcon 9, Falcon Heavy, Dragon, and Starship, Rocket Lab Electron, ULA Atlas and Vulcan, and Stratolaunch Roc and Talon. The transition itself does not change the 180-day statutory clock or the pre-application requirement, but it means new entrants and modifications now run exclusively under Part 450 and its Advisory Circulars.

faqs.question: Where do AI agents help with Part 450 work?
faqs.answer: The structured, document-heavy nature of Part 450 maps cleanly onto agent workflows. The highest-leverage applications are compliance matrix generation against each Subpart C and D requirement, drafting means-of-compliance proposals under 14 CFR 450.35 grounded in the relevant Advisory Circular, building flight hazard analyses per AC 450.109-1, drafting system safety program documentation per AC 450.103-1, parsing FAA RAIs and routing them to the responsible subsystem owner, and maintaining a single source of truth between the CONOPS, trajectory, debris, and toxic models. The work still requires a licensed safety engineer of record, but agents materially shorten the iteration loop that the Commercial Space Federation described as "endless back and forth."

h1: How long does an FAA Part 450 launch license take?
meta_description: FAA Part 450 launch license timeline: the 180-day statutory clock, real durations from Astra, New Glenn, Starship, and where pre-application, RAIs, and NEPA slip it.
meta_title: FAA Part 450 launch license: how long does it take?

sections.heading: The statutory 180-day clock
paragraphs: The headline number for an FAA Part 450 vehicle operator license is 180 days. It comes from 51 U.S.C. 50905(a)(1), which directs the Secretary of Transportation to "issue or transfer a license" within 180 days of accepting an application if the applicant complies. 14 CFR 413.15 incorporates that statute into the procedural rule and adds a tolling provision: if an accepted application later turns out to be missing information needed to continue review, the FAA tolls the clock until the information arrives. The statute also requires written notice of unresolved issues within 120 days, and 14 CFR 413.15 requires written notice if the FAA misses the deadline.

Two details matter for any builder reading the 180-day number. First, the clock only starts at acceptance under 14 CFR 413.11, not at filing. The FAA must notify the applicant in writing of either acceptance and initiation of review, or rejection with each reason and the action required. AC 413.13-1, issued December 18, 2023, is the FAA's own definition of what counts as a complete application. Second, pre-application consultation under 14 CFR 413.5 is mandatory, uncapped, and entirely off the clock. FAA Associate Administrator Kelvin Coleman has cited an average post-acceptance issuance of 151 days and a roughly 98 percent on-time rate, both measured from acceptance forward.

Part 450 itself took effect March 10, 2021 under the Streamlined Launch and Reentry final rule (SLR-2), collapsing the legacy 14 CFR Parts 415, 417, 431, and 435 into a single performance-based framework. The five-year transition window closed March 9, 2026; the FAA confirmed full transition in a March 2026 newsroom release.

sections.heading: The real timeline, broken into phases
paragraphs: Total wall-clock time from program kickoff to a Part 450 license is the sum of four phases. Only the third phase is on the 180-day clock.

subsections.heading: Phase 1: pre-application consultation
paragraphs: Mandatory under 14 CFR 413.5. Driven by the FAA Part 450 Pre-Application Consultation Checklist, which scopes the concept of operations, vehicle and payload hazards, trajectory parameters, program schedule, proposed means of compliance, license scope, safety element approvals, incremental review requests, and a compliance plan mapping each Part 450 requirement to a deliverable. AST runs this under internal procedure P-011 in the AST Safety Management System Manual.

Reported duration: months to years. The Congressional Research Service (R48582, 2025) wrote that "some industry stakeholders have reported that the process may range from months to years" and identified no published average. Commercial Space Federation president Dave Cavossa told the House Science Committee on September 10, 2024 that pre-application has been "an endless back and forth process" lasting "years" for some companies.

subsections.heading: Phase 2: means-of-compliance acceptance
paragraphs: Under 14 CFR 450.35, five categories of means of compliance must be accepted by the Administrator before the application can be accepted: flight safety analysis methods under 450.115(b)(1), toxic hazards for flight under 450.139(e)(1), highly reliable flight safety system under 450.145(b), lightning hazard mitigation under 450.163(a)(1), and ground toxic hazards under 450.187(e)(1). The FAA maintains a public table of accepted MoCs at faa.gov/space. This phase frequently runs in parallel with pre-application and is the single largest cause of upstream RAI churn.

subsections.heading: Phase 3: the 180-day statutory review
paragraphs: After acceptance under 14 CFR 413.11, the FAA conducts policy review under 14 CFR 450.41, payload review under 14 CFR 450.43, safety review under 14 CFR 450.45, and environmental review under 14 CFR 450.47, with interagency consultation involving DoD, State, and NASA. The statutory deadline is 180 days; the average from FAA self-reporting is 151 days; the published low-water mark is 114 days for Blue Origin's New Glenn (December 27, 2024 issuance).

subsections.heading: Phase 4: post-license obligations and modifications
paragraphs: Subpart D obligations begin at issuance: the licensee must operate per representations made, comply with Subparts C and D, and route any change through the modification process under 14 CFR 413.17 and 14 CFR 450.211. Modifications can themselves be material consumers of calendar; SpaceX's Starship has cycled through repeated mods since VOL 23-129 issued April 14, 2023.

sections.heading: What slips it: RAIs, means of compliance, environmental review
paragraphs: The FAA's 98 percent on-time figure is measured from acceptance forward and excludes the period where most calendar is actually consumed. The drivers below are where the time goes.

subsections.heading: Requests for Additional Information
paragraphs: An RAI is a formal request for documentation or analysis the FAA considers necessary to continue review. There is no published per-applicant RAI count, but Deputy Associate Administrator Michael O'Donnell told a House subcommittee in November 2023 that 4 of the first Part 450 licenses required "substantial iterations." Every Part 450 applicant has effectively received multiple RAI rounds in pre-application and at least one formal round after acceptance.

subsections.heading: Means of compliance ambiguity
paragraphs: Part 450 is performance-based. Where an Advisory Circular has not yet defined an accepted method, the applicant proposes one, the FAA asks for substantiation, and the cycle repeats. Former AST head Wayne Monteith called this "ambiguity in what it takes to meet the requirements." The FAA issued 17 Advisory Circulars in the year preceding the September 2024 House hearing and targeted 10 more, including AC 450.131-1 (Probability of Failure, August 2025) and AC 450.169-1A (Collision Avoidance Analysis, September 2025).

subsections.heading: NEPA environmental review
paragraphs: Under 14 CFR 450.47, the FAA decides on a categorical exclusion, written re-evaluation, Environmental Assessment, or Environmental Impact Statement. Scope changes reset analysis. SpaceX publicly attributed the slip of Starship Flight 5 from August to October 12, 2024 to Endangered Species Act Section 7 reconsultations with NMFS and USFWS triggered by hot-stage ring and tower-catch profile changes. The Starship Programmatic EA ran from a September 17, 2021 draft to a Mitigated FONSI and Record of Decision on June 13, 2022; the Tiered EA for expanded operations ran roughly 18 months from public draft to the May 6, 2025 FONSI authorizing up to 25 launches per year and RTLS profiles.

subsections.heading: AST workforce constraint
paragraphs: GAO-24-106184 (February 21, 2024) and CRS R48582 document that AST has been under authorized headcount in 8 of the past 9 years. As of August 2024, AST had 158 staff with 65 to 70 percent on licensing. SpaceX VP Bill Gerstenmaier warned the Senate in October 2023 that the "entire commercial regulatory system is at risk of collapse" without doubled AST funding and expedited hiring authority.

sections.heading: Real Part 450 timelines, by operator
paragraphs: Public dates and durations for issued Part 450 licenses, drawn from FAA newsroom releases, operator press releases, and primary reporting.
bullets: Astra Rocket 3.3 (LV0008), CCSFS LC-46: first ever Part 450 license, issued February 4, 2022. Application work began mid-2021; total roughly 6 to 8 months. Source: Astra press release; Spaceflight Now.
SpaceX Starship VOL 23-129, Boca Chica: issued April 14, 2023, six days before IFT-1 on April 20, 2023. Five-year term. Source: FAA Starship stakeholder page.

SpaceX Starship modifications: IFT-2 license modification November 15, 2023; IFT-3 March 13, 2024; IFT-4 June 4, 2024; IFT-5 October 12, 2024 (hardware-ready in early August per SpaceX). Source: spacenews.com/faa-defends-starship-licensing-delays.
SpaceX Starship Tiered EA expansion: FONSI and ROD May 6, 2025 authorizing up to 25 launches per year and RTLS profiles. Source: CNBC May 6, 2025.
Varda W-1: first Part 450 reentry license. Initial reentry denied September 6, 2023; reentry license granted February 14, 2024; reentry executed February 21, 2024. Source: SpaceNews.
Blue Origin New Glenn: Part 450 vehicle operator license issued December 27, 2024, in 114 days from acceptance. First flight January 16, 2025. Source: Spaceflight Now; FAA newsroom.

Rocket Lab Neutron: cleared the Part 450 preliminary acceptance gate by August 8, 2025; FCC license granted August 2025; targeted Part 450 issuance by end of 2025. Source: TechCrunch July 22, 2025; satellitetoday.com August 28, 2025.

Relativity Terran R: pre-application and application active in 2025 to 2026; no vehicle operator license issued as of mid-2026; first flight targeted late 2026 from LC-16. Source: nasaspaceflight.com June 19, 2025.
Stoke Space Nova: SLC-14 site approval October 20, 2024; $510M Series D September 23, 2025; no Part 450 vehicle operator license publicly announced as of mid-2026.
Aggregate: 8 Part 450 licenses issued and 1 denied as of June 2025 (CRS R48582); 14 Part 450 licenses issued by the March 9, 2026 transition deadline (FAA).

sections.heading: How to compress the timeline
bullets: Start pre-application consultation under 14 CFR 413.5 as early as the concept of operations is stable. The clock you cannot reset is the one you start late.
Work the FAA Part 450 Pre-Application Consultation Checklist as the agenda for every meeting and deliver each item in writing.

Map every Subpart C and D requirement to an existing Advisory Circular: AC 450.103-1 (System Safety Program), AC 450.109-1 (Flight Hazard Analysis), AC 450.115-2 (Flight Safety Analysis), AC 450.117 (Trajectory), AC 450.123 (Population Exposure), AC 450.131-1 (Probability of Failure), AC 450.139-1 (Toxic Hazards), AC 450.145 (Highly Reliable FSS), AC 450.165 (Flight Commit Criteria), AC 450.167-1 (Tracking), AC 450.169-1A (Collision Avoidance), and AC 450.173-1 (Mishap Plan).

File proposed means of compliance under 14 CFR 450.35 early. The five categories that require Administrator acceptance before application acceptance are 450.115(b)(1), 450.139(e)(1), 450.145(b), 450.163(a)(1), and 450.187(e)(1).
Build the application to AC 413.13-1 so acceptance under 14 CFR 413.11 succeeds on the first pass and the 180-day clock starts cleanly.
Request incremental review under 14 CFR 450.33 if your safety case decomposes into modules that can be reviewed independently and in workable chronological order.

Front-load NEPA scope under 14 CFR 450.47 to cover the full flight envelope you actually want, not just the maiden flight. The Starship Flight 5 slip is the canonical case of a late scope change triggering Section 7 reconsultation.
Track and respond to RAIs within days, not weeks. 14 CFR 413.15 tolls the 180-day clock the moment the FAA flags missing information.

sections.heading: 2025 to 2026 reforms: SpARC, Executive Order 14335, transition close
paragraphs: Three reform threads now sit on top of Part 450 and will shape any application opened in 2026 and beyond.

The Launch and Reentry License Requirements Improvement Aerospace Rulemaking Committee, known as the Part 450 SpARC, was chartered by Transportation Secretary Pete Buttigieg on November 7, 2024 and announced November 14, 2024. Its scope covers nine topic areas including flight safety analyses, system safety, means of compliance, treatment of reentry vehicles, and treatment of vehicles still in flight test. The consensus final report was delivered on its late-summer 2025 schedule. As of June 2026 the FAA has not published a Notice of Proposed Rulemaking implementing the recommendations.

Executive Order 14335, "Enabling Competition in the Commercial Space Industry," was signed by President Trump on August 13, 2025. It directs the Secretary of Transportation, within 120 days (deadline December 11, 2025), to reevaluate, amend, or rescind Part 450 requirements not justified by actual launch or reentry risk; to consider exemptions for vehicles with flight termination or automated flight safety systems and for vehicles holding FAA airworthiness certificates; to broaden reliability criteria for reentry vehicles; and to eliminate or expedite environmental reviews not required by statute. A follow-on order, "Ensuring American Space Superiority," signed December 2025, reinforces the deregulatory posture.

The five-year Part 450 transition window closed March 9, 2026. The FAA confirmed full transition on March 17, 2026. Operators that transitioned by the deadline include Blue Origin (New Shepard and New Glenn), Firefly (Alpha), SpaceX (Falcon 9, Falcon Heavy, Dragon, and Starship), Rocket Lab (Electron), ULA (Atlas and Vulcan), and Stratolaunch (Roc and Talon). The transition close means new entrants and modifications now run exclusively under Part 450 and its Advisory Circulars.

Adjacent context: the FAA Reauthorization Act of 2024 (P.L. 118-63, signed May 16, 2024) treats commercial space operations as routine NAS users and expands AST workforce authorities. The Commercial Space Launch Amendments Act human spaceflight learning period was extended from January 1, 2025 to January 1, 2028 via Section 5702 of the FY2025 NDAA. The orbital debris NPRM from September 26, 2023 was formally withdrawn January 15, 2026.

sections.heading: Where AI agents materially help on Part 450
paragraphs: Part 450 is a performance-based regime built on a corpus of structured documents: 14 CFR Parts 413 and 450, a growing library of Advisory Circulars, the Part 450 Pre-Application Consultation Checklist, AC 413.13-1, internal FAA procedures, and the applicant's own CONOPS, trajectory, debris, toxic, and reliability models. The work that consumes calendar is iteration between those documents and the FAA reviewer's questions. That iteration loop is what agents shorten.

The highest-leverage applications are: building and maintaining a compliance matrix against each Subpart C and D requirement; drafting proposed means of compliance under 14 CFR 450.35 grounded in the relevant Advisory Circular; producing flight hazard analyses per AC 450.109-1 and system safety program documentation per AC 450.103-1; parsing incoming RAIs and routing each to the responsible subsystem owner with the cited regulation, AC paragraph, and prior commitment in context; and maintaining a single source of truth across the CONOPS, trajectory, debris, and toxic models so that a late scope change does not silently invalidate an accepted MoC.

The work still requires a licensed safety engineer of record and an Authorized Safety Element Approval signer. The point is not to remove the human; it is to compress the loop the Commercial Space Federation called "endless back and forth" so the 180-day clock, once it starts, is the binding constraint rather than the volume of paperwork between rounds.
